item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Use the metric system. Use of Imperial is what I consider the worst tone problem in SCPs. Another thing: The main goals of the Foundation are to Secure, CONTAIN, Protect and MAINTAIN NORMALCY. I am pretty sure that paying people with pieces of an anomalous woman is not containing nor is it maintaining normalcy. This is such an opposite to the Modus Operandi of the Foundation that it is perfectly plausible that the O5s would personally beat up whoever authorized these tests… Just a nitpick, but she is sapient, must be fed and attempted to breach containment at least once, that's Euclid.
-1.

item.title: Containment procedures
item.content:encoded:
Had to downvote simply because of the Object Class. A humanoid can never be "safe". She's Euclid and should be changed ASAP. If this little change is met I can upvote with pride.

Great article besides this flaw.

item.title: Re: HEY tag Team!
item.content:encoded:
Around here, it's quite common to put a single dollar on display at small businesses. I thought it was that in the article. The slot machine apparently took it, so that points towards currency, too. On the other hand, it holds substantially different value between two individuals in the same city, which should not be true of a currency.

What makes it currency to me is that it holds value despite both parties recognizing it as intrinsicly useless, but I admit that there's room for debate. (That's why I called it out publicly instead of PM'ing, I was worried this might be one of those things you get from every confused user)

item.title: Re: HEY tag Team!
item.content:encoded: "Exchange" is more appropriate, as evidenced by the way the body parts are treated like something that isn't just money (such as when the piece of skin, in the first experiment log, is put on display in the pawn shop).

item.title: Re: HEY tag Team!
item.content:encoded:
There's no currency involved.

(or, alternately, we're fallible humans.)

item.title: HEY tag Team!
item.content:encoded:
Why is this not tagged "currency?"

also, +1 for interesting and weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea improves with a complete reading of the article, but I think it is missing the unsettling feeling you usually get from a good SCP. The neat writing is a plus point, though.

A kinda reluctant +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great SCP, but what ever happened to sample 1177-50?

item.title: Reaching out to the photographer
item.content:encoded:
With Voct's encouragement, I reached out to the photographer who had been wondering why this one portrait got more traffic than anything else he'd done in that album. I posted as thothmes1 to his flickr page for the photo here: https://www.flickr.com/photos/65337134@N00/7210587324

Hello fsendel! I am part of the community that writes for the SCP Foundation wiki at www.scp-wiki.net

We are a collaborative fiction-writing project that has inspired many games, video series, and stories. This particular photo has been used for one of our more popular entries which you can find at the URL below. Thank you for releasing this lovely portrait via Creative Commons - one of our authors was very taken with it. If you want to comment on the circumstances under which you took the photo, we'd LOVE to hear about it at the discussion forum page which is linked at the bottom of the article.

Many thanks.

www.scp-wiki.net/scp-1177
"The Coupon Cutter"

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
This SCP squicked someone out hard, and they requested I make a reading:

https://youtu.be/tfiBZmTH5oM

Hope ya'll enjoy it!

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at anytime.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

So, the effect here in SCP-1177 is very similar to the effect portrayed by Mr. Money in SCP-2855, with the difference being the amount of the anomaly needed for transaction. While 1177 can use strips of her flesh as currency, SCP-2855 is traded whole. This would suggest that one may be a more refined version of the same anomalous effect than the other. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-1177 is a Latin-American female of indeterminate age who possesses the ability to exchange severed pieces of her own body for goods and services. Although she appears elderly, this may be a result of her long-term abuse of her anomalous property and lifestyle—interviews reveal that, prior to entering Foundation custody, she had spent much of her adult life as a homeless vagrant. SCP-1177 was discovered in the [REDACTED] casino in Kansas after she bribed the doorman into allowing her entry and made several attempts to play a nickel slot machine without the use of casino tokens before being apprehended by security and delivered to paramedics. Foundation agents alerted to these events posed as employees of a local mental health facility and were given custody of the woman without incident. The slot machine and the rug under it were submitted for cleaning.

To this:

SCP-1177 is a Latin-American female of indeterminate age who possesses the ability to exchange severed pieces of her own body for goods and services1. Although she appears elderly, this may be a result of her long-term abuse of her anomalous property and lifestyle—interviews reveal that, prior to entering Foundation custody, she had spent much of her adult life as a homeless vagrant. SCP-1177 was discovered in the [REDACTED] casino in Kansas after she bribed the doorman into allowing her entry and made several attempts to play a nickel slot machine without the use of casino tokens before being apprehended by security and delivered to paramedics. Foundation agents alerted to these events posed as employees of a local mental health facility and were given custody of the woman without incident. The slot machine and the rug under it were submitted for cleaning.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Similarity between this ability, and that possessed by SCP-2855 has been noted. Investigation into a common source of both phenomena, or if one is a variant of the other, is ongoing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd say that initial attempt at a containment breach was when she'd only been in custody for a month or so, before she realized how much better being an SCP is, compared to living on the streets and selling her blood, skin, and flesh.

As an SCP, she gets fresh food, she gets medical checkups, she gets regular access to a shower, she gets a bed without any vermin, she gets clean clothes, she gets magazines that're only a few months old, she gets a private toilet, and she doesn't have to mutilate herself to pay for any of it.

I mean, sure, the doctors will take little pieces of her skin and hair and muscle sometimes, but they use anesthetic and disinfectant!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think most of this is good, but I'm going to down vote and heres why: The Containment Procedures. For one, pretty much any humanoid should be classed Euclid because you can't just lock them in a box and ignore them - they need to be fed and monitored. Plus, she's demonstrated her sneakiness and ability to maim herself without being caught before, and there should be steps taken to keep from doing that other than just watching and alerting the researcher. She should have hand-restraining mittens and a Hannibal Lecter-style mouth cage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What really gets me about this one is that it seems to be affecting the Foundation. A lot of the experiments and tests just seem unnecessarily redundant and cruel. How many samples have been taken for study, somewhere in the seventies? And many of the tests seem to be repeated.

It's almost as if the Foundation is seeing this lady as another form of currency: information. In my headcannon, researchers are being affected in such a way that they think more experiments will lead to even more information, despite how similar the results usually are. And they keep doing it anyway, when all actual evidence should be telling them "Welp, we've seen enough, lets put some more safety measures in place in order to keep her safe from other people."

It's very subtle, but that's where the horror is coming from for me, on this one. Not sure if that was the actual intent, but hey, take my +1 anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And just how are they supposed to test the SCP to make sure that their understanding is correct? Or seeing how long it takes before they are inert (which would effect how they go abour cintainment)?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 for idea itself, but I don't like idea of Foundation trading anomalous items away. It is opposite of containment.

"As of 08/10/██, the piece of skin is framed and on display in one of the shop's cabinets."
Nice containment breach there, guys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I bet it was accidental. For example, she lost finger or something in accident and one of paramedics said "Hey, that's neat. I will give x$ for this finger.".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't usually like to compliment things that wig me out this bad, but take your damn +1. What the actual fuck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did too! If I hadn't seen the time stamp I would have thought someone was inspired by those commercials.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The researcher was given three dimes and one nickel (value $.0.35) in change.

This was the moment when I stopped reading, scrolled to the rating module, pressed +1, and continued reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Anyone seeing the new anti-smoking commercials?

http://m.youtube.com/watch?v=ixMEEI0Zq9g

http://m.youtube.com/watch?v=Ks2L6XFLAeA

Immediately thought of this SCP when I saw them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Body horror has a special squick value for me. This is one of those that just had me cringing for the whole article. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With the permission of Silber, I found an image that is licensed under Creative Commons.

https://flic.kr/p/bZb9p7 Link to original artist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You just hit on what I was thinking. For something simpler than a skin graft, does a blood transfusion dilute the "value" of her blood? If so, does the value return at the normal rate of blood replacement, or is it faster (showing that new blood somehow assimilates)?
If a graft of her skin was put on a suitable donor, would the effect eventually assimilate the new person? Would the effect vanish?

On a too-silly note, might her body accept a graft of paper currency? Might a sufficiently demented researcher try?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since her flesh has been in circulation for at least 6 months and still retains value, does that mean that her flesh is immune to decomposition or are people trading rotten meat with each other? Also, is she immune to pain or just that badass to regularly chop pieces of herself off? And how does her anomalous properties affect flesh transplants, if you gave her a skin graft would the new skin eventually acquire "value" of it's own? What if you cloned her skin in a petri dish, would the cloned flesh have the same "valuable" property?

item.title: Photo source
item.content:encoded:
For the record: here's the source of the photo.

http://www.flickr.com/photos/lennyfurman/1254776775/in/set-72157594501710578/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eeeeughhhhh! Definitely one of the grossest SCPs I've read, but also funny in a really morbid way. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this one a lot. I didn't know what to expect when I started reading it, was told it was about a woman who sold her flesh, so I thought it might be about a prostitute or someone who can exchange their body parts for someone else's parts. Nope, this is a crazy old lady who can give parts of her body to people and they treat it like money. The more I read the stranger things got, but in line with the theme, building upon itself as it went on. At the end, the idea that they experimented with using her flesh, buying stuff in the Foundation, buying stuff outside the Foundation, and finally tracking the flesh as it made rounds in the world. Oh man, that is strange! o_0

Really liked this one. Also, I'll have to start handing all my money to homeless guys to check over from now on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This, except maybe even worse. The homeless man recognized it; so what if this is actually quite common among homeless and transients? There could be much more in circulation than we thought, with people all over the world paying for goods and services with the flesh of the homeless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The thing that made this article for me was the last experiment log. If you think about it, pieces of her flesh could be in circulation and we would have no idea. You could end up paying with some lady's skin, and no know about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not really that creepy, but it's like a better alternative to selling your body to science.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The slot machine and the rug under it were submitted for cleaning.

This seems oddly specific and fairly unimportant. I don't really see why the slot machine or rug would be mentioned if not taken into foundation custody unless it was something like "The slot machine and the rug under it were not deemed necessary for Foundation acquisition"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"It appears you need a kidney transplant, ma'am."
"How much will that cost?"
"Oh, about 20,000 dollars."
*pulls out defective kidneys, hands them to doctor*
"I'll get your change right away, Ma'am."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahh remember reading this when it was first posted and just got rerouted here by the recent tale. +1 from me, definitely glad that I don't have my nervous habit of sucking coins any more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ewewewewew! -wretches- +1, for making one of the few articles that disturb the hell outta me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really gives a new meaning to "legal tender."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to have to agree with Voct on this one; all scientific measurements, globally, are in Metric. I'm upvoting because this article is some creepy gross amazing shiznits, but please consider changing 'inches' to centimeters.

… oh god. I wonder if her excrement— WAIT, NO, WE WILL NOT THINK OF SUCH THINGS IN THIS LABORATORY.

Here's five bucks on a job well done.

…Um, shit, I've only got one strip of flesh and a tooth. Take this for now and I'll give you the rest later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upped. I felt like a little kid reading this. I just said "Ewwww" while giggling the whole time.

Gross, crazy, and intriguing. Magnificent. Good to see people dancing just outside the comfort zone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: best name for a club ever, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1177-52: Placed in a dancer's top at the [REDACTED] gentleman's club.

… I think I just threw up a little.

Upvote for that and for the general horror.

… uch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ayyyyyyy!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My upvote was this article's to lose from the first sentence of the description. And it didn't.

Nicely done. +1'ed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really fucking good, on a lot of levels. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"$4.50 for a cup of coffee! I'm not made of money here!….oh, wait."
"I'd give my right arm for a decent martini! Of course, I'd expect some change back."
"This apartment costs me an arm and a leg. I don't know how I'm going to pay for the third month."

Jokes aside, I like it. Really viscerally appaling without being overdone gory. A few questions:

Her flesh is accepted as alternative payment, not as cash per se. So why do people give change and accept it in situations where alternative payment isn't acceptable? I picture it as beign like a gift certificate, but you don't get change if you use a $5 gift certificate to buy a $4 sandwich. And there is no stripper I'v ever heard of who would let you stick a coupon/voucher in her g-string.

Since you pointed it out, what did happen to sample 50?

How did she ever figure this out herself? From the test log we know that the bits are perceived as flesh unless they are presented as payment, so the first time this happened she would have had to offer a bit of flesh as payment without knowing it would work. Now, that is something a really crazy person might do. But then we are left with a delusional person whos delusion came true, which just isn't as good a concept. Some sort of back-story might help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fucking hell, this is revolting. +1.

Also:

…[SCP-1177] bribed the doorman into entering and made several attempts to play a nickel slot machine without the use of casino tokens […] The slot machine and the rug under it were submitted for cleaning.

This is an excellent example of how to leave the horror to our imaginations without resorting to crude expungement. Nowhere does it actually say anything explicitly gory, but you sure can fill in the blanks yourself… Augh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. The concept is what usually a bad article is but the exection is PERFECT.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
METRIC.

USE METRIC, DAMMIT.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damnit, Sal, you even make articles you had nothing to do with funny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just like how she's pointing at the camera like the Fonz. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really brilliant, says I.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably none, it's not that her flesh looks like money, it's that it holds some kind of intrinsic value.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jesus christ. How much of this is already in circulation? How much is in your wallet right now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this idea and the execution. Also the tracking of the flesh is cool to me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well done, upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The key word here is "confiscate". It's like… Medical marijuana. If you've got an approved testing regiment, you go down to the storage room, grab some, do the test, report the findings. You're NOT allowed to go down, get some, stick it in your pocket, and walk out the front door with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea of it, masochistic to get stuff. You've got an up vote for me, it's hard to make me revolted o frightened, but judging by the other comments I can safely say that you've done good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Technically, the containment section should say how they prevent her from gathering up pieces of hair that just fall out (like in a comb/brush), but that might be too nit-picky.

Any staff member who attempts to confiscate any section of SCP-1177, whether a sample taken for experimentation or from SCP-1177 herself,

So how does a researcher go about conducting experiments? Or are further tests forbidden?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think they should have gotten change back from buying NFL Superpro. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept, but it seems wrong that the Foundation would let fleshmoney stay in circulation like that — the pieces are anomalous objects in their own right.

The stripper and the collection plate were particularly vivid images.
